days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment was mostly mixed-neutral on April 27, with Copilot and Gemini tied at the top in a close call, OpenAI and Anthropic tied below, and several stories splitting between mild praise and criticism [E4][E3][E1][E2].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot ranked tied for first in a close tie with Google Gemini, but the balance leaned negative on pricing complaints and concerns about higher experimentation costs [E4][E5][E6][E7].
Google Gemini also tied for first in a close tie, though support was low and the discussion was mostly neutral with one comment criticizing Gemini Nano versus newer Gemma models [E3][E8].
OpenAI tied with Anthropic for third in a close tie, with neutral coverage centered on the Microsoft deal ending and a comment calling the new terms friendly but still mixed overall [E1][E2].
Anthropic tied with OpenAI for third in a close tie, with mild praise for Opus 4.5’s helpfulness offset by a comment dismissing Claude as attention-grabbing [E2][E9][E10].

evidence.excerpt: Microsoft and OpenAI end their exclusive and revenue-sharing deal

annotations.rationale: The title is a factual business announcement about OpenAI ending an exclusive revenue-sharing arrangement, without clear praise or criticism.

evidence.excerpt: This agreement feels so friendly towards OpenAI that it's not obvious to me why Microsoft accepted this. I guess Microsoft just realized that the previous agreement was kneecapping OpenAI so much that the investment was at risk, especially with serious competition now coming from Anthropic?

annotations.rationale: The commenter says the new agreement is 'friendly towards OpenAI' but also says the previous deal was 'kneecapping OpenAI', yielding mixed sentiment overall.
Calling Anthropic 'serious competition' frames it as a credible competitive threat, which is mildly favorable.

evidence.excerpt: Interesting side effect of this is that Google Cloud may now be the only hype scaler that can resell all 3 of the labs models? Maybe I'm misinterpreting this, but that would be a notable development, and I don't see why Google would allow Gemini to be resold through any of the other cloud providers.

annotations.rationale: The comment speculates about Gemini resale strategy across cloud providers but does not clearly praise or criticize Gemini itself.

evidence.excerpt: GitHub Copilot is moving to usage-based billing

annotations.rationale: The title reports a billing-model change for GitHub Copilot without evaluative language.

evidence.excerpt: Why should I pay $10 and be forced to use it by the end of the month, while I can pay $10 and have it last as long as I want?

annotations.rationale: In the Copilot billing story context, this is a clear complaint that the new pricing structure is worse for the user.

evidence.excerpt: Well. Just got an email from GitHub saying they'll be raising prices for Co Pilot. "To keep up with the way you use Copilot, we're transitioning to usage-based billing, and we want to give you enough time to prepare." Man, it was fun. Having my tokens subsidized by Microsoft. If the prices go up to much I guess I'll try Deepseek again.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Copilot is raising prices and suggests switching away if prices rise too much, indicating dissatisfaction.

evidence.excerpt: The part that worries me is that this market is still very early. Most developers and organizations are still learning how to use these tools effectively. Raising the experimentation cost this much may slow down the discovery process that makes the tools valuable in the first place.

annotations.rationale: Within the Copilot pricing discussion, the commenter says the higher experimentation cost is worrisome and harmful to adoption.

evidence.excerpt: This looks like it uses Gemini Nano under the hood. But the latest Gemma4 E2B and E4B models appear to be much better, so you'd probably be better off deploying quantized versions through an extension for now. - Gemini Nano-1: 46% MMLU, 1.8B - Gemini Nano-2: 56% MMLU, 3.25B - Gemma4 E2B: 60.0% MMLU, 2.3B - Gemma4 E4B: 69.4% MMLU, 4.5B

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Gemini Nano appears worse than alternative models and recommends using something else instead.

evidence.excerpt: > I can't take all of the credit. My little robot intern (Opus 4.5) has been very helpful with the busy work, leaving me free to handle the trickier planning and implementation. ;)

annotations.rationale: The quoted text says Opus 4.5 was 'very helpful' and freed the author for harder work, which is mild praise.

evidence.excerpt: These interactive tutorials used to be better when they were made by hand to demonstrate the concept accurately. This Claude stuff just feels like an attention grab.

annotations.rationale: The commenter dismisses 'Claude stuff' as inferior and merely attention-seeking, a clear negative judgment.

days.headlineSummary: HN sentiment was most negative toward Microsoft Copilot on weak support, while OpenAI drew mixed-to-negative enterprise-access criticism and Anthropic stayed mixed overall despite some strong pro-access comments [E6][E7][E1][E2][E5].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot ranked most negative on low support, with commenters calling it the weakest mainstream coding offering and blaming Microsoft prioritization for decline [E6][E7].
OpenAI was negative overall, driven by complaints about poor Azure/corporate access and low trust in enterprise settings, partially offset by Bedrock-based access as a compliance unlock [E1][E2][E3].
Anthropic landed mixed-neutral with high volume: some praised Bedrock and trusted-access adoption, but others criticized Claude Code for harming code quality and teamwork [E1][E2][E5].

evidence.excerpt: Availability through Bedrock has been a major driver in use of Anthropic in my org. And I am betting there is actual margin in it as well. I wonder if this is directly linked to the split up with Microsoft. Just from my anecdata, OpenAI is getting completely ignored in serious enterprise deployments because what they offer on Azure sucks and there is no other corporate friendly way to get it. They probably saw themselves getting destroyed in enterprise and realised it was existential to be able to compete with Anthropic on AWS.

annotations.rationale: Says Bedrock availability has been a major driver of Anthropic usage in the commenter’s organization.
States OpenAI is being ignored in enterprise because its Azure offering 'sucks' and lacks a corporate-friendly access path.

evidence.excerpt: Claude got a looooot more buy in with a lot of privacy-concerned orgs I work with because they could access it through their "trusted" intermediate Amazon. OpenAI has been banned and is not trusted. I'm not sure that I agree with these orgs' legal teams' assessments, but they definitely read the terms of service far closer than I did. We will see if this changes the equation, but it feels like OpenAI is pretty far behind and playing catch up on all fronts. Though to be honest, "pretty far behind" is like 2-8 weeks in the AI world, so it may not matter a ton, it's mostly perception. And for me and my information bubble, perception of OpenAI is rock-bottom due to Sam Altman. From appearing unethical to appearing unhinged with demands from fabs and everything else, I'm not a fan.

annotations.rationale: Says Claude got much more buy-in from privacy-concerned organizations because access through Amazon felt trusted.
Says OpenAI is banned, not trusted, behind on all fronts, and has rock-bottom perception due to Sam Altman.

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI on Bedrock basically lets those orgs skip the separate DPA negotiation with OpenAI. Could be a bigger unlock than it looks on paper.

annotations.rationale: Frames Bedrock access to OpenAI as a meaningful compliance/privacy unlock by avoiding a separate DPA negotiation.

evidence.excerpt: Who owns the code Claude Code wrote?

annotations.rationale: The title is a legal/IP question about Claude Code without clear praise or criticism.

evidence.excerpt: I think that the gold rush approach happening right now around me (my company EMs forcing me to work with claude as fast as possible) show really short-sight of all the management people. First - I lose my understanding of the code base by relying too much on claude code. Second - we drop all the good coding practices (like XP, code review etc.) because claude is reviewing claude's code. Third - we just take a big smelly dump on the teamwork

annotations.rationale: Strongly criticizes Claude/Claude Code as harming understanding, coding practices, and teamwork.

evidence.excerpt: Copilot, though, has been consistently the weakest mainstream AI coding offering. Inferior to Cursor or Windsurf at editor completions, inferior to Codex, Claude, OpenCode, blah blah blah, at agentic coding and also the old-school chat-style... And now, it's no longer cheap AND now sucks even more than it has all along — the new $39/month plan is not only worse than all its competitors, but worse than its own $10 plan was a month ago — by a lot.

annotations.rationale: Directly calls Copilot the weakest mainstream coding offering and says the new plan is worse and more expensive.
Says Copilot is inferior to Codex, implying Codex is better on coding tasks.
Says Copilot is inferior to Claude, implying Claude is better on coding tasks.

evidence.excerpt: the switch from being independent to being part of Microsoft, having resources pushed to Copilot instead of core service, the organization structure itself, a reliance on vibe coding, etc etc.

annotations.rationale: Criticizes prioritizing Copilot over GitHub’s core service as part of GitHub’s decline.

days.headlineSummary: Anthropic drew the most negative sentiment, while OpenAI was mildly criticized and Microsoft Copilot received one positive anecdotal report, all on low support [E2][E1][E3].

ranking.summary: Anthropic ranked most negative on a single strongly critical comment about rising costs and perceived hypocrisy, so this is low-support [E2].
OpenAI was discussed negatively in one comment that interpreted its ad move as a sign of financial strain, but the signal is low-support [E1].
Microsoft Copilot had one positive anecdote describing Copilot CLI successfully helping with a Godot game and fixing regressions, but support is low [E3].

evidence.excerpt: So, is this OpenAI announcing they're strapped for cash?

annotations.rationale: Directly names OpenAI and frames the ad move as a sign of financial weakness.

evidence.excerpt: This one sided type of embedded insurance is not unique to Anthropic, but sharply increasing cost, layered on top of the self righteousness, seems to be making the stench unbearable over the past year.

annotations.rationale: Explicitly criticizes Anthropic's costs and behavior in highly condemnatory language.

evidence.excerpt: I've been doing this lately, building a Godot game with Copilot CLI. I'm using Godot MCP Pro which can automate interactions and screenshots, and have the whole game script in a markdown doc. I was happily surprised when I asked for a walkthrough and it all just worked, found and fixed some regressions while I was sleeping.

annotations.rationale: Describes Copilot CLI as working well in practice and successfully finding and fixing regressions.
